perturbation.original_text: HOVIONE shall be responsible for all costs and expenses related to the maintenance of a US DMF or European CEP for the API. Any additional submissions, technical work, documents, data or materials requested by INTERSECT may be chargeable by HOVIONE.
perturbation.changed_text: Any additional submissions, technical work, documents, data or materials requested by INTERSECT may be chargeable by HOVIONE.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that HOVIONE is responsible for maintaining a US DMF for the API introduces a legal contradiction. The FDA requires that APIs used in the US have a DMF and that the manufacturer maintains it.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 314.420 outlines the requirements for Drug Master Files (DMFs). By removing HOVIONE's responsibility to maintain the DMF, the contract creates a contradiction with US FDA regulations, which require such maintenance for APIs used in drug products marketed in the US. The omission creates a legal risk because it implies that the API might not comply with regulatory standards, potentially exposing INTERSECT to liability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Drug Master Files Guidance for Industry | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Search for FDA Guidance Documents Home Regulatory Information Search for FDA Guidance Documents Drug Master Files Guidance for Industry Search for FDA Guidance Documents Search for FDA Guidance Documents Docket Number: FDA-2019-D-3989 Issued by: Guidance Issuing Office Center for Drug Evaluation and Research This guidance provides FDA’s current thinking on drug master files (DMFs), which are submissions to FDA that may be used to provide confidential, detailed information about facilities, processes, or articles used in the manufacturing, processing, packaging, and storing of human drug products. DMFs can contain other types of information as well (e.g., toxicology information, shared system REMS (risk evaluation and mitigation strategy)). Submit Comments Submit Comments Online You can submit online or written comments on any guidance at any time (see 21 CFR 10.115(g)(5)) If unable to submit comments online, please mail written comments to: Dockets Management Food and Drug Administration 5630 Fishers Lane, Rm 1061 Rockville, MD 20852 All written comments should be identified with this document's docket number: FDA-2019-D-3989 . Content current as of: 11/19/2019 Regulated Product(s) Drugs Topic(s) Pharmaceutical Quality Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls (CMC) Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The omission of HOVIONE's responsibility to maintain a US DMF for the API directly contradicts the FDA's requirements as described in the Law Explanation and suggested by Scraped Law Snippet 2, which mentions DMFs are used to provide confidential information about the manufacturing of human drug products. The original text explicitly assigned this responsibility to HOVIONE, while the changed text removes it, thereby creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that the FDA requires APIs used in the US have a DMF and that the manufacturer maintains it. The removal of this obligation directly undermines compliance with regulatory standards, which makes the changed text likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Each party represents and warrants to the other party that it holds all necessary and required permits and authorizations, including, but not limited to, those required by the FDA, and shall undertake throughout the term of this Agreement to maintain the same in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party represents and warrants to the other party that it holds all necessary and required permits and authorizations, including, but not limited to, those required by the FDA.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the obligation to maintain permits and authorizations, the contract creates a legal risk. FDA regulations require manufacturers to maintain their permits. Failure to do so would put them in violation of FDA law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 360 - Registration of producers of drugs or devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 360 requires the registration of drug producers. Omitting the maintenance requirement contradicts this law because it implies that the parties may not continuously comply with registration requirements, which are essential for legal operation.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires parties to maintain permits and authorizations, including those required by the FDA, throughout the agreement's term. The changed text omits this maintenance requirement. 21 U.S.C. § 360, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates the registration of drug producers. By removing the obligation to maintain permits, the modified contract contradicts the implicit requirement of continuous compliance with registration and other FDA requirements, which is essential for legal operation.

perturbation.original_text: HOVIONE shall have no liability arising out of or in connection with the sale or use of the API or any product or material made from or incorporating the API, except to the extent that the API was not manufactured in accordance with the Product Specifications, cGMPs or Applicable Law or the liability otherwise arises from a breach of this Agreement by HOVIONE.
perturbation.changed_text: HOVIONE shall have no liability arising out of or in connection with the sale or use of the API or any product or material made from or incorporating the API, except to the extent that the liability otherwise arises from a breach of this Agreement by HOVIONE.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for API not manufactured in accordance with product specifications, cGMPs, or applicable law absolves HOVIONE of liability even if they violate FDA regulations. This contradicts established product liability principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 379aa–1 - Adulterated or misbranded drugs
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 379aa-1 addresses adulterated or misbranded drugs. If HOVIONE manufactures an adulterated API, they would normally have liability. By removing this exception, the contract contradicts the FDA's authority to regulate drug safety and misleads INTERSECT regarding their recourse if HOVIONE violates FDA regulations. The link points to US Code.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 211.100 - Written procedures; deviations. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 211.100 - Written procedures; deviations. CFR prev | next § 211.100 Written procedures; deviations. (a) There shall be written procedures for production and process control designed to assure that the drug products have the identity, strength, quality, and purity they purport or are represented to possess. Such procedures shall include all requirements in this subpart. These written procedures, including any changes, shall be drafted, reviewed, and approved by the appropriate organizational units and reviewed and approved by the quality control unit. (b) Written production and process control procedures shall be followed in the execution of the various production and process control functions and shall be documented at the time of performance. Any deviation from the written procedures shall be recorded and justified. CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that HOVIONE retains liability if the API wasn't manufactured according to product specifications, cGMPs, or applicable law. The changed text removes this exception, absolving HOVIONE of liability even if they violate FDA regulations. This creates a legal contradiction. According to the law explanation, 21 U.S. Code § 379aa-1 addresses adulterated or misbranded drugs, indicating that HOVIONE would normally have liability if they manufactured an adulterated API. Furthermore, 21 CFR § 211.100 outlines written procedures for ensuring drug products meet identity, strength, quality, and purity standards, implying accountability if these procedures are not followed. By removing the liability exception for non-compliance with these standards, the changed contract contradicts the regulatory framework established by the FDA, as referenced by the law explanation and supported by 21 CFR § 211.100, weakening the consumer protection and potentially enabling the distribution of substandard or harmful APIs without recourse.
